data.title: Hercules Capital: Growing Troubles Within Portfolio Warrants Caution
data.description:
Overview

Business Development Companies continue to be my favorite place to get a mixture of capital appreciation and high income. However, the current macroeconomic environment has reduced the appeal of the sector. Many BDCs have started to experience a drop in portfolio quality and lower earnings as a result of the higher interest rate environment. I recently published an article titled 'Warning To All BDC Investors', which I would recommend you go back to read for a more specific breakdown of my concerns. I was lucky enough to initiate a position in Hercules Capital (NYSE:HTGC) around the $12 per share mark, and recently sold out of that position at around $19 per share.

With enough of a decline, HTGC is one of the high-quality BDCs that I would initiate a position in again if valuation was attractive. On a YTD basis, HTGC's price is still down nearly 10%, but I have concerns that the price can continue its slide towards $16 if interest rates aren't cut. The price still remains above its average price to NAV level, indicating that HTGC is still overvalued. However, it's hard to come across a high-quality BDC at a discount opportunity, so there's also a chance that we've already seen the worst and conditions improve from here. Non-accruals have remained basically flat, with only 1 new addition.

If internal performance does improve, there's certainly a chance that we could see a turnaround. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HTGC's price has declined by about 8.2%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return lands at a positive 1.2%. The main appeal of holding a position in HTGC is the generous starting dividend yield of about 10.5%. This double-digit yield makes it an attractive option for investors who have an objective of creating a sizable stream of dividend income. So let's start by taking a look at the current risk profile for HTGC.

Risk Profile

One of the biggest sources of risk that I am cautious about is the growing demand for private credit investments, such as BDCs. Demand is growing from both institutional and retail investors, which may seem like a great thing for the long-term growth of the sector. However, I worry that the demand outpaces the amount of high-quality deals that are available in the market. Sourcing highly profitable and sustainable deals in this higher interest rate environment is challenging enough.

This increased demand means there is increased competition for fund managers. I interpret this increased competition as many different hands reaching into the same pot of higher-quality deals. Ideally, BDCs want to continue growing their base assets, NAV per share, and net investment income year over. I fear that some BDCs may start to loosen their underwriting criteria just to secure some new growth opportunities, which can lead to higher non-accruals in the long term if interest rates remain elevated.

I tend to monitor the changes in non-accrual rates every quarter for all of the BDCs in my portfolio. I believe that non-accruals provide us with an insight into management's underwriting ability, and the portfolio's interest rate sensitivity, and help us get an idea of how management addresses its issues. Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers who are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. At the time of my last coverage, non-accruals amounted to 1.7% of cost and 0.5% of fair portfolio value.

The latest earnings call revealed that non-accruals have remained mostly flat, with non-accruals of 1.8% of cost and 0.5% of fair portfolio value. This can be attributed to one additional company being added to non-accrual status. Management stated that they feel confident in the resilience of their portfolio for the remainder of the year. However, I wanted to highlight one interesting point during that earnings call. Management states that many companies are freezing decision-making as they await better certainty about the outlook of the macroeconomic environment. I believe that this may be the source of risk that can drive non-accruals higher at some point.

What we've noticed, particularly over the last 30 to 60 days, is that companies are sort of freezing decision-making given the uncertainty. So decision-making with respect to potential growth investments, decision-making with respect to exploring strategic options, decision-making with respect to capital raise, those discussions, those decisions have slowed down. And I think a lot of companies are just looking for clarity and looking for certainty from a policy perspective. We think that will change hopefully relatively quickly, but that's something that we are watching pretty closely. - Scott Bluestein, Chief Executive Officer

While non-accruals don't paint the entire picture, I wanted to provide some points of reference. Therefore, I listed some of the non-accrual rates of peer BDCs below with their respective total return over the last twelve months. The rest of the BDC sector has seen a slight uptick in non-accruals, which is just the nature of having a portfolio of debt investments that are structured on a floating rate basis.

  • Main Street Capital (MAIN): 1.7% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • PennantPark Investment (PNNT): 1.5% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX): 1.2% non-accrual rate at fair value.

We can see that HTGC has underperformed its peers over the last twelve months, indicating that there could be some other flaw related to its portfolio strategy. I believe it is related to the growing amount of PIK interest income, which I will circle back to later.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, HTGC has total investments at the fair value of $3.9M that are allocated across a diverse range of industries. Looking at the pie chart breakdown below, you may strain your eyes trying to figure out what color matches what percentage. I saved you the effort and provided a breakdown of the top five industries below.

  • Software: 32.2%
  • Drug Discovery & Development: 28.5%
  • Healthcare Services, Other: 15.9%
  • Consumer & Business Products: 9.6%
  • Electronics & Computer Hardware: 4.3%

What I gathered from the breakdown is that a significant portion of HTGC's assets are concentrated in the top three sectors, which make up 76.6% of assets when combined. The software and healthcare sectors tend to be more volatile and sensitive to interest rate changes. Since HTGC is one of the few BDCs that include venture debt as part of its portfolio, the risk in these sectors is elevated. Unfortunately, the higher interest rate environment has slowed down the appeal of venture lending due to the elevated interest expenses.

98% of HTGC's portfolio is structured on floating rate debt investments, which allowed for a greater amount of interest income to be collected at one point. While this may have been beneficial in prior years, the burden of the higher cost of debt is beginning to weigh down on borrowers. For reference, the federal funds rate still remains near its decade highs, despite a few rate cuts happening during Q4 of 2024. The Fed has decided to leave rates unchanged during their latest meeting, and I anticipate that interest rates will remain elevated for the remainder of 2025. The full impact of President Trump's tariffs is still to be determined, and the Fed will likely continue to monitor inflation data over the next few months.

On a more positive note, 90.9% of the portfolio is structured on first-lien senior secured loans. First-lien senior secured debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure, which means this debt has the highest priority for repayment. This reduces the chance that HTGC loses all invested capital in a bad transaction. In a scenario where a borrower defaults on their loan and has to liquidate assets, HTGC's loans will be entitled to repayment before other forms of debt.

Lastly, HTGC's consistent performance and track record of success have caused the price to trade at one of the highest premiums to NAV valuations in recent years. As we can see below, the price currently trades at a premium to NAV of 1.74x, indicating that entry here may not be ideal. Wall Street analysts have an average price target of $20.11 per share, indicating a potential upside of nearly 11%. However, I believe the price may retract back down to the $17 range before making its way back up, due to some underlying concerns.

Q1 Earnings — Worrisome PIK Interest Income

HTGC reported its Q1 earnings at the beginning of May and the results were a bit mixed in my opinion. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.45 per share, which was a 10% reduction from the prior year's Q1 earnings. Pre-tax net investment income landed at $77.4M, which was also a slight 2% decrease from the prior year's amount of $79.1M. Total net operating expenses did slightly decrease by 1% to $42M, from the prior year's total net operating expenses of $42.3M.

However, the thing that really stood out to me was the massive 36% increase in PIK (payment-in-kind) interest income. PIK interest income increased to $13.4M, up from the prior year's total of $9.8M. This is an issue for me because PIK interest income can be defined as interest that is accrued but not actually paid in cash from a borrower. This means that borrowers are having a more difficult time paying their interest in cash. Therefore, HTGC is allowing payments to be deferred and adding the interest to the principal of their loans instead. HTGC is legally allowed to categorize this as interest income, even though no actual cash was received.

Granted, HTGC may be excused by many optimistic investors, since their whole investment approach exposes the BDC to many cash-constrained businesses in the early startup stage. However, I tend to shy away from increased risk profiles like this because I believe this issue can build in the background and surprise investors when conditions become much worse. If PIK's interest income is increasing at this rate, it could mean that the distribution will eventually be in jeopardy of a cut. Imagine a scenario where interest rate cuts do not happen for another twelve months; how will HTGC be able to offset this deterioration if there are a limited number of deals in the market?

Despite HTGC's net assets slightly increasing above $2B, the NAV per share continues to decline quarter over quarter. A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that a problem may be arising.

  • Q1 2024 NAV: $11.63 per share
  • Q2 2024 NAV: $11.43 per share
  • Q3 2024 NAV: $11.43 per share
  • Q4 2024 NAV: $11.66 per share
  • Q1 2025 NAV: $11.55 per share

As we can see below, the net interest margin percentage continues to shrink as a result of the deterioration. Profitability is being threatened by a higher cost of funding and a lower yield collected from its portfolio. While the margins still remain healthy, I just thought it was noteworthy to mention the trend of its margin.

On a more positive note, HTGC was able to originate total gross debt and equity commitments of over $1B and gross fundings of approximately $539M during the quarter. This was the second-highest gross funding that HTGC was able to achieve in its entire history. Even after sales and repayments, the portfolio saw a net growth of about $270M during the quarter. Despite the troubling increase in PIK interest income, I do like the fact that management seems to be very active in trying to resolve weak areas.

The focus of our origination efforts in Q1 and quarter-to-date Q2 was on maintaining a disciplined approach to capital deployment with an emphasis on diversification and avoidance of certain sectors that we believe will be more challenged in the current operating environment. Our Q1 originations activity placed a slightly greater emphasis on technology companies versus life sciences companies. - Scott Bluestein, Chief Executive Officer

Dividend Support

According to the most recently declared quarterly base distribution of $0.40 per share, the current dividend yield sits at around 8.8%. Management also declared a supplemental distribution of $0.07 per share over the last quarter, bringing the total payout to $0.47 per share. As previously mentioned, net investment income for the last quarter amounted to $0.45 per share. So while this does still fully support the base distribution, I wouldn't expect a significant number of supplementals to be paid over the next twelve months.

HTGC has built a strong reputation around the consistency of its dividends. Looking at the visual below, we can see how the payouts have remained fairly stable and reliable, despite shifts in the overall macro environment. Therefore, I anticipate that management will do their best to keep the distribution the same and avoid any cuts. As it currently stands, net investment income levels support the base distribution with a coverage rate of 112.5%. Since HTGC recently allocated a large amount of capital towards new investment activity, I anticipate this coverage margin to improve once these new investments start to materialize. Therefore, I am not really concerned about a dividend cut at this moment.

Even if there aren't any formal base dividend raises or supplementals in the future, this consistency must be admired. That's because long-term investors can essentially create their own dividend growth by reinvesting a portion of the income received from HTGC.

It's worth mentioning that the distributions received from HTGC are made up of net investment income. Therefore, all distributions received will be classified as ordinary dividend income. Based on the amount of capital invested, specific tax bracket, or desired income level, a position in HTGC may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on HTGC at this moment due to the premium valuation and some underlying concerns about the portfolio quality. Although non-accruals remained relatively unchanged, PIK interest income has increased. This could be an indication that underlying borrowers are having a hard time making the cash interest payments needed. If interest rates remain elevated for longer than anticipated, this could cause the quality of the portfolio to worsen. However, management allocated a large amount of capital towards new investments, which could materialize into stronger earnings throughout the remainder of the year. The distribution is still supported by earnings, alleviating concerns about a cut.

data.title: Shurgard Self Storage: Attractive Growth Profile, But Too Expensive
data.description:
Introduction

Shurgard Self Storage (OTCPK:SSSAF) is a Belgium based REIT focusing on self-storage facilities across Europe. When the company initially completed its IPO, I decided against participating, as I just didn’t see Europe following the ‘American style’ of using self-storage services. But it appeared to have been a case of ‘build, and you will be able to find customers’ as Shurgard (and other self storage REITs in Europe) have done quite well. As the stock has come off its 52-week high, I wanted to have another look at Shurgard to see if it now meets my investment criteria.

Shurgard Self Storage has its primary listing in Belgium, where it is trading on Euronext Brussels with SHUR as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is almost 42,000 shares. The current market capitalization is approximately 3.5B EUR, as the REIT has approximately 98.5M shares outstanding. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

The REIT continues to grow

Shurgard does not provide detailed quarterly financial statements, but its Q1 trading update clearly shows the direction the REIT is heading in, while the full-year guidance remains very strong as well.

During the first quarter of the year, the revenue increased by approximately 19.5% and thanks to additional efficiency and economies of scale, this allowed the NOI to increase by 20.4% to 64.6% as the NOI margin increased from 57.5% to 57.9%. The EPRA earnings, which are comparable to the FFO result in North America, came in at 35.7M EUR or 36 cents per share. That’s an increase of just 3.2% compared to the same quarter a year before, but that is related to the increased debt level and interest expenses as the REIT remains committed to its substantial investment program.

As the table above shows, the REIT has almost four dozen expansion projects in the pipeline for a total investment of 515M EUR. Of that amount, about a third will be spent this year. The addition of 212,000 square meters will add about 13% to the net rentable square meters that the REIT currently has available.

The outlook for 2025 remains stable: the REIT expects an 11% NOI increase (this appears low given the sharp increase in the first quarter, but the REIT’s H2 2024 was much stronger than the first half after completing an acquisition) and a 0.5% improvement of the underlying EBITDA margin. The net interest expenses are expected at approximately 50M EUR.

This means we can now use this guidance in combination with the 2024 results to derive the anticipated earnings. We know the 2024 NOI was approximately 268M EUR, and an 11% increase results in an anticipated NOI of 298M EUR.

Last year, the G&A expenses were 28M EUR, and after deducting about 35M EUR in G&A and the 50M EUR in anticipated interest expenses, the pre-tax income should come in at around 213M EUR. Applying the average tax rate of 18.5% would result in an attributable FFO of 174M EUR and divided over the 98.5M shares outstanding, this implies EPRA earnings of 1.77 EUR per share. That’s in line with the consensus estimates.

The earnings should continue to increase at a mid to high single digit percentage, and I expect the REIT to reach EPRA earnings of close to 2 EUR per share in 2027.

Is this how Public Storage wants to increase its European footprint?

The REIT has a relatively small free float of just 28%, and two shareholders own almost 70% of the capital. As you can see below, the New York State Common Retirement Fund owns just over a third of the stock, while Public Storage (PSA) owns almost 35% of Shurgard’s share.

Shurgard went public in 2018 when it was a private company mainly held by the New York retirement fund (with a stake of 50.9%) and Public Storage which owned 48.9%.

The question now is what the main shareholders would prefer to do. At the current share price of approximately 36 EUR, Shurgard is trading at close to 20 times its FFO which is in line with its peers (Public Storage is trading at a forward FFO multiple for 2026 of 17 while smaller peers with a market cap of respectively $10B and $4B (the latter is more in line with Shurgard’s market capitalization) CubeSmart (CUBE) and National Storage Affiliates (NSA) are trading at forward multiples of 16 and 14 respectively). Applying a multiple of 15 times earnings to Shurgard would result in a target price of just 28 EUR by the end of 2026.

It could make sense for Public Storage to take Shurgard private again, perhaps together with the New York State retirement fund, but at the current valuation, the stock may be a bit in limbo. Shurgard is perhaps a bit too expensive to try to acquire it (as a 20-25% premium would push the multiples to 23–24 times next year’s FFO result) but it’s also not expensive enough to consider selling given its growth profile and the fact that PSA is trading at similar (and even slightly higher) multiples. Public Storage may continue to use Shurgard as its platform to build out a European subsidiary, but I doubt the US-based REIT has any plans to take Shurgard private again.

Investment thesis

While I underestimated the demand for self-storage units in Europe, I’m still not planning to go long anytime soon. At the current share price of just over 36 EUR per share, the self-storage REIT is trading at about 20 times next year’s earnings and even if it reaches the 2 EUR per share in FFO result by the end of 2027, the stock still wouldn’t be cheap at 18 times the FFO result.

As such, I remain on the sidelines and am a ‘hold’ on Shurgard Self Storage.

data.title: TEAF: Divesting Private Investments As Fund Transforms
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Tortoise Sustainable and Social Impact Term Fund (NYSE:TEAF) is a closed-end fund that holds a unique portfolio split between private and public investments, primarily in the energy space. However, after a strategic review of the fund due to a lack of performance over the years and activist pressure, they decided to divest the private holdings.

Since announcing that shift in March of this year, they've made significant progress on reducing the private investment sleeve. That said, the fund's performance since announcing the transition has been pressured. This was due to an overall volatile April and a weaker general environment for energy investments.

Since our last update, the fund's total returns have been slightly positive. The update came only a week or so before the announcement of the results of their strategic review.

TEAF Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.99
  • Discount/Premium: -12.51%
  • Distribution Yield: 9.25%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.98%
  • Leverage: 14.88%
  • Managed Assets: $211.655 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date around March 27, 2031)

TEAF was launched with the goal of "attractive total return potential with emphasis on current income and uncorrelated assets." Additionally, "access to differentiated direct investments in essential assets" and "investments in intangible, long-lived assets and services." TEAF is also targeting a "positive social and economic impact."

The fund is leveraged, and that increases the potential for higher returns but also for amplifying downside moves. Overall, the fund is rather mildly leveraged relative to most CEFs. Still, it also adds further expenses through the borrowing costs. That pushes the fund's total expense ratio to 2.75%.

Private Divestment As Fund Repositions

They announced their "next steps" after the strategic review of TEAF on March 17, 2025. The announcement was that they'd be looking to divest most of their private investments as they felt that was in the best interest of shareholders.

As part of this review, TEAF engaged Lucid Capital Markets to facilitate the sale of its private credit, social infrastructure, assets. The fund is currently under contract to divest these holdings, as it was determined that divestment is in the best interest of shareholders. As of March 17, 2025, these private credit assets comprised 29% of TEAF’s portfolio. The sale of the private credit assets is expected to close on or about March 20th generating proceeds of $55.9 million.

In addition, TEAF engaged Marathon Capital to facilitate the sale of its fixed renewable assets. The fund is currently under exclusivity for the sale of the majority of the private renewables portfolio, with the transaction expected to close in Q2 2025. The proceeds from the sales will be reinvested in TEAF’s listed energy infrastructure holdings. The sustainable infrastructure holdings will remain unchanged.

Private investments can be quite mixed; they can provide a significant boost in overall results in an ideal world, but just like the public world, not everything is a hit. Further, the process brings with the the "true value" of the underlying portfolio. As private level 3 securities, the valuations of these investments are generally a best estimate based on various valuation techniques.

At the end of the day, though, they are really only worth what someone is willing to pay for them—and that price tag isn't figured under a negotiation that is settled between the parties.

Since the announcement of the divestment of their private holdings, mostly linked to renewable energy or credit investments, TEAF has actually performed a bit better than the traditional peers on a total NAV return basis. That includes the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and Tortoise Essential Energy Fund (TPZ), a newly formed ETF with the merger of its previous CEF sister funds. TEAF had also outperformed its remaining CEF sister fund, Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp (TYG).

However, on the other side of this, it significantly underperformed its renewable-focused peer ETFs, iShares Global Clean Energy ETF (ICLN) and Invesco Solar ETF (TAN). So it appears that they were divesting their mostly renewable-focused private investments at a time that looks like it was advantageous to be allocated to those types of holdings.

To be fair, they still hold some of their private investments, as they account for 17% of the fund's portfolio as of May 31, 2025. These are some of the largest renewable investments they had through the Renewable Holdco positions.

Further, we had an incredibly volatile April 2025 that would have mired the performance of TEAF and its peers through this period as well. That can make it a bit more cloudy to come up with a solid conclusion on whether they were getting the valuations they had assigned to their private positions or not. In the next report, due in a couple of months, we might be able to get a better idea of how accurate the valuations might have been.

TEAF has also seen its discount narrow since our last update, and it has narrowed quite materially from the deep-discounted levels we saw in 2023/2024.

Ultimately, only time will tell if this was the correct decision to go back to a more traditional public infrastructure-invested portfolio. Albeit, one that is still focused on "sustainable" infrastructure that can make it a little bit more unique relative to its sister funds and peers.

Impact On Monthly Distribution

Even after the fund is repositioning itself through a transformation, the fund has continued to pay the same monthly distribution. At the $0.09 per month, that works out to a distribution rate of 9.25%—and thanks to a significant discount to NAV, the fund's NAV rate comes to 8.09%.

In terms of distribution coverage, we may actually see net investment income decline. This would be due to the divestment of its private credit portfolio, which would have generated a significant portion of the total investment income.

However, the fund still always required meaningful capital gains to support the payout as well. NII for fiscal 2024 came to only 37%, and including the MLP return of capital distributions, distributable cash flow coverage came to 47%.

In this case, from the top ten, we see that now Energy Transfer LP (ET) has moved from a 2.7% allocation to 9.1%. MPLX LP (MPLX) also saw a significant increase from 2.7% to 4.9%. ET pays a distribution rate of 7.18%, and MPLX pays a distribution rate on its units that currently works out to 7.41%.

With this transition, it would appear that the managers are going to be trading in interest payments for distributions characterized as return of capital. That could result in the NII coverage decreasing, but DCF coverage not seeing as significant an impact. Therefore, the overall coverage of the distribution in terms of what could be considered relatively stable recurring cash flow versus the need for capital appreciation may not see a massive shift—that's despite the overall transformation of the portfolio.

Conclusion

TEAF has made the decision to exit much of its private portfolio after its strategic review came to a conclusion. The manager believes this is in the best interest of the shareholders over the long term as the fund becomes more focused on public securities in the infrastructure space. Of course, only time will tell if this is ultimately the right decision that will lead to long-term success. This also means that TEAF isn't so unique anymore, though they still hold what would be considered a rather sizeable tilt toward sustainable infrastructure through primarily its Renewable Holdco investments.

For now, TEAF remains an interesting fund in terms of what is still a rather sizable discount and attractive distribution yield. Saba Capital still owns a sizeable stake in the fund at around 11.33%. They actually even bumped up their allocation during the broader April market swoon.

data.title: PGIM Muni High Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
PERFORMANCE RECAP

For the quarter, the PGIM Muni High Income Fund Class Z (MUTF:PHIZX) returned -0.07% net of fees and outperformed the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index and the Bloomberg Customized Blend Index gross of fees.

KEY CONTRIBUTORS

  • An overweight to Industrial Development Bonds (IDBs) and Pollution Control Revenue (PCR).

  • The Fund’s exposure to Puerto Rico sales tax securities.

KEY DETRACTORS

  • The Fund’s yield curve positioning.

  • The Fund’s exposure to specific finance projects.

MARKET REVIEW

In Q1, the total return performance for tax-exempt municipal bonds was negative, led by longer dated maturities. This left the muni yield curve flatter by quarter end. In addition, at the March FOMC meeting, the Fed rates held steady, amid policy changes. 2025 GDP growth was revised lower (to 1.7% from 2.1%) and core PCE inflation was revised higher (to 2.8% from 2.5%).

The tax-exempt market, as represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, returned -0.22% in Q1 (on a total return basis), while the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Index returned 2.99%. Municipal/Treasury yield ratios were higher across the curve, with the 5-year, 10-year, and 30-year ratios ended the period at 72%, 77%, and 92%, respectively.

Gross issuance in March totaled $43.6 billion ($40.2 billion in tax-exempt and $3.4 billion in taxable issuance), representing a 17% increase over march of 2024. This brought gross issuance for Q1 2025 for tax-exempt municipal bonds to $111.4 billion, second only to gross issuance in Q1 2027 (J.P. Morgan).

On the demand side, tax-exempt mutual bond funds posted outflows of approximately $1.1 billion in March, led by outflows from IG munis. Meanwhile, inflows for the quarter were positive, totaling approximately $9.8 billion.

QUARTER

The Fund’s overweight to IDB & PCR contributed to performance. In addition, the Fund’s exposure to Puerto Rico sales tax securities contributed to performance as sale tax collections on Island continued to be strong. In addition, security selection in housing contributed to performance.

The Fund’s security selection in charter schools detracted from performance. In addition, exposure to two project finance deals had to be written down due to updated project recovery assumptions.

The Fund’s duration positioning had minimal impact, but the Fund’s curve flattener positioning detracted from performance as the 5s20s curve steepened.

PORTFOLIO OUTLOOK

In Q1, munis underperformed U.S. Treasuries. The muni yield curve -particularly the long end- steepened significantly. This has been a function of retail demand for shorter-dated bonds outpacing demand for longer-dated bonds. In addition, heavy supply has skewed towards the longer end of the curve.

The year-to-date divergence in performance between Treasuries and munis is unusual as the performance of both normally move in the same direction. However, from a rates perspective, underperformance of the long end of the curve could create a buying opportunity as this ratio potentially moves higher.

On the technical side, as the probability of a Fed cut continues to grow, issuers may hold off in the immediate term in the hopes of getting lower rates in the future. This would help to mitigate higher supply in Q2. In addition, $20B in reinvestment capital is expected in May.

Muni credit fundamentals remain strong, with healthy balance sheets, although credit fundamentals have likely peaked given ongoing challenges emanating from the Trump administration. While fundamentals remain strong, the main concern is the knock-on effects of slower growth. That stated munis are generally viewed as a late cycle “safe haven.”

In terms of positioning, airport spreads within IG have tightened. We have been selling into that strength and raising cash for potential dislocations in the market (e.g., during weeks of heavier supply). In addition, we are avoiding credits that are less susceptible to Medicaid cuts-such as healthcare providers, which have higher Medicaid payer mixes, or universities that have high leverage, weak balance sheets, and/or are reliant on endowment revenue.

Barrons: PGIM Investments ranked 4 out of 48, 8 out of 47, 12 out of 46 firms for the 1-, 5- and 10-year periods ended 12/31/2024, respectively. See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. PGIM Muni High Income Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the High Yield Municipal Debt Funds category were: 109/188, 51/182, 57/176, and 47/116, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund’s respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 3/31/2025: 91/190, 48/179, 72/173, and 47/114, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

AVERAGE ANNUAL RETURN AS OF 3/31/2025

Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than original cost. For the most recent month-end performance go to PGIM Investments.

Gross operating expenses: Class Z, 0.62%. Net operating expenses, 0.58%, after a contractual reduction through 12/31/2025. Expenses are as of the most recent prospectus. Net operating expenses reflect expenses after fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements by PGIM Investments, if any. data.title: Oil Price Hike: 2 Big Dividend Stocks Set To Spike
data.description:
As most of you are probably aware by now, Israel has launched what it views as a preemptive strike on Iran's nuclear facilities as well as members of its military and scientific leadership in an attempt to prevent it from developing a nuclear weapon. First and foremost, I am praying for a quick end to the conflict and that peace, both near term and long term, will be restored to the Middle East. With that being said, and putting on my investor cap, if this conflict becomes a drawn-out war between Israel, Iran, and potentially other parties, here are two big dividend stocks that could benefit immensely.

Big Dividend Stock #1

The first opportunity is energy production company Civitas Resources (CIVI). It currently pays out a very attractive dividend of $2 per year, which means that its current yield is 6.4% per year. Additionally, its stock trades at a massive ~51% discount to its net asset value, which management has been capitalizing on by buying back stock fairly aggressively this past quarter.

Moving forward, the big question for the company is, can it reduce its leverage sufficiently to get to a point where it can then shift its immense free cash flow generation towards buying back stock and thereby forcing the market to recognize the significant intrinsic value embedded in the company? However, if energy prices continue to flounder, it may not be able to generate significant enough free cash flow to meet its debt reduction objectives in the near term. It also may not find it to be an opportune time to sell assets to try to generate additional cash proceeds to pay down debt.

As a result, it may eventually find itself in a position where the dividend could be in jeopardy, or at the very least, its ability to return cash to shareholders will be significantly limited beyond the base dividend for a prolonged period of time. However, with oil prices spiking by about 10% (as of this writing) in the wake of Israel's strike on Iran, this could be a major tailwind for the company that will enable it to meet its debt reduction goals. This is because as an energy producer, its profits are highly leveraged to the price of oil. Therefore, with oil prices skyrocketing, the company may be able to generate significant free cash flow to meet its debt objectives in the near term. While this same dynamic applies to virtually every other oil producer out there, in CIVI's case, this is especially beneficial because the market has discounted and beaten down its share price so heavily recently over concerns about lower oil prices, which have an outsized impact on its value proposition due to its higher leverage.

Big Dividend Stock #2

The other opportunity I think could soar higher, albeit less than CIVI will, is Plains All American Pipeline (PAA) (PAGP). PAA is a midstream infrastructure company (AMLP), which means that it has relatively limited commodity price exposure over the near term. In fact, based on its latest investor presentation, Plains only has about 20% exposure to commodity prices. Therefore, the recent spike in oil prices will likely not have a huge uplift on its near-term profitability.

However, it generates 85% of its EBITDA from its crude oil segment, and therefore its unit price has suffered due to the recent weakness in oil prices, as the market for whatever reason tends to price midstream stocks in line with the direction of their related energy commodities, even though their profitability is only impacted a very little bit over the near term by these price swings. Therefore, it is logical to expect that as a major oil infrastructure business, soaring oil prices should help PAA substantially and push the price higher moving forward.

Meanwhile, you also get a very attractive distribution yield of about 9% on a forward-looking basis along with an investment-grade balance sheet, very low leverage of just 3.3 times, and the potential to grow its distribution at a double-digit CAGR for the foreseeable future.

Additionally, if the fight between Israel and Iran does become a long, drawn-out conflict that materially moves energy prices higher, it could cause a significant production increase in oil in the United States, which would be a big benefit to PAA as increased volumes through its pipelines would increase its fee revenue and boost its profits. Meanwhile, if the war spreads and causes economic weakness in the United States as well—especially due to higher oil prices potentially weighing on economic activity and pushing inflation—PAA is very defensively positioned due to its highly contracted nature and its significant investment-grade counterparty exposure, which would make it a stable, defensive play in the face of an economic slowdown.

Investor Takeaway

While CIVI is certainly a much more aggressive upside play than PAA is, and in fact has much more upside potential in the near term than many of its larger, more blue-chip peers like Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), it also is a higher-risk play due to its higher leverage and the fact that if this war ends up pushing the world into a recession, energy prices could eventually plummet, which would hurt CIVI quite a bit. Meanwhile, PAA also offers greater upside potential than many of its peers that are less heavily exposed to oil, such as Kinder Morgan (KMI), Enbridge (ENB), and even Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Energy Transfer (ET). At the same time, however, it is more defensively positioned than a company like CIVI is due to its highly contracted nature and only 20% exposure to commodity price volatility.

Therefore, I think that both of these options are attractive ways to profit from the surging geopolitical turmoil in the Middle East—especially in major oil-exporting countries like Iran—while also providing some balance between direct commodity price exposure and more limited commodity price exposure, even if PAA is also positioned to benefit from improving market sentiment. Fortunately, we have been positioning our portfolio to benefit from this sort of volatility recently, and as a result, we are heavily overweight in the energy sector with several even more attractive risk-adjusted opportunities than CIVI and PAA at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Sigma Lithium: Low Costs Make It Ideal For The Next Lithium Upswing
data.description:
Sigma Lithium Corporation (SGML) is a green lithium producer with mines located in Brazil. Its proprietary dense media separation [DMS] technology and tailings-recycle enable industry-leading recoveries and unit costs. Unfortunately, over the past 18 months, there's been a 25% surge in global spodumene supply in 2024, with another 15% forecasted for 2025. As a result, Lithium benchmark concentrate prices have come down from $830 per tonne to $612 per tonne, and carbonate spot prices are roughly 20% lower YTD. This glut pressures near-term revenues, potentially reducing full-year sales by 10%-15% at sustained $9,000 per tonne of carbonate. However, SGML's low cost, optionality to withhold volumes, negotiate floor-price offtakes, and preserve cash until market recovery are good news for long-term bulls.

Lithium Macro Headwinds

Sigma Lithium Corporation is a vertically integrated lithium mining and processing company with a focus on sustainable practices of hard-rock lithium production in Brazil. SGML operates the Grota do Cirilo open-pit pegmatite mine in Minas Gerais, with 107 million tonnes of reserves at 1.4% lithium oxide. The company uses proprietary dense media separation [DMS] to produce the crushed, separated, dried ore that is called spodumene concentrate, upgraded to ore with a 5-6% lithium oxide. I've previously covered SGML in April 2025, where I basically took a bullish stance on the stock.

While I did mention the macro risks, it's unfortunate that the worst-case scenario on that front played out. The ongoing glut in Lithium supply hasn't improved enough. There's still a high likelihood of at least a thin supply surplus for the remainder of 2025, which probably translates into further macro headwinds for SGML. Still, I believe the worst part of those macro headwinds should last until 2025, and may even swing into a slight deficit by 2026. If that's the case, I would argue that 2025 is probably around the "macro bottom" for SGML's underlying commodity prices.

Sigma's Low-Cost Edge in a Down-Market

To give you an idea, over the past eighteen months, the lithium market has transformed, with capacity additions in Australia, China, and South America. The global spodumene supply grew around 25% in 2024, with a further 15% expansion forecast for 2025. This supply has outpaced end-market demand, especially in energy-storage systems. As a result, benchmark Chinese spodumene prices fell from late 2024 to around $620 by May 2025. C2 equivalent refers to spodumene concentrate that grades at 5% lithium oxide.

Similarly, lithium carbonate prices in China have retreated over 20% to about $8,145 per tonne. Still, remember that SGML is one of the world's lowest-cost hard-rock lithium producers. So, while SGML still has positive cash gross margins of 35%, these are undoubtedly harsh headwinds that force it to accept lower market prices in the near term. Also, I wouldn't consider these types of margins in "distressed" territory by any means. And in fact, over the long run, this macro situation could be an opportunity because SGML is low-cost, flexible, and can withhold uncommitted volumes.

Management mentioned they can do this until prices recover modestly, then negotiate prepayments or floor-price offtake agreements using their ESG credentials. Additionally, SGML can schedule and stagger investment spending, like the Plant 2 funding, to match market conditions and cash-flow needs. So, overall, there's still plenty of room for maneuvering, operationally speaking. SGML also noted they met their Q1 2025 production target of 68,300 tonnes of battery-grade lithium concentrate. SGML loaded 29,000 tonnes to International Resource Holdings [IRH], adding to the previous 76,000 tonne total shipment.

With Q1 2025 output sold into the spot or short-term contract market, average realized prices have trended below the company's original budget assumptions. But, fundamentally speaking, the company has delivered on what it can control itself. In fact, my original thesis on SGML was that they'll reach and sustain their 270 kt pa output by Q4 2024. So far, this has been the case. Additionally, their BNDES loan largely de-risks their Phase 2 construction cash needs, and as of their latest update, construction is on schedule. After all, this loan is mostly back-end-loaded starting in 2027, so in the short term, it isn't a major cash drain for SGML.

I also imagined their costs would trend lower, likely below the $660 per ton level. This has also played out as management anticipated, as SGML's Q1 showed their costs are around $622 per ton. Naturally, what hasn't played out is the macro variable, namely, Lithium prices have remained weak. And as you might imagine, this has kept SGML's valuation multiples depressed (more on this later).

Furthermore, SGML's Q1 2025 produced 68.3 ktonnes of battery-grade spodumene concentrate, and 62 ktonnes of that was sold into spot and short-term contract markets. The remaining 6.3 ktonnes were added to inventory. This does show they can stockpile a bit until prices recover. Also, SGML's all-in sustaining cost [AISC] CIF-China of $622 per tonne in Q1 2025 already places them in the lowest quartile of the global hard-rock cost curve. So, in that sense, they seem to be in an ideal spot to weather the storm. But obviously, in the near term, the sustained price declines are going to remain the largest headwind. After all, $9,000 per tonne of carbonate, or $3,200 per tonne of spodumene equivalent, will definitely compress margins and reduce free cash flow.

It's also worth mentioning that it's not all bad news for SGML. Their proprietary DMS technology actually delivers plant-level recoveries are over 70%. Also tailings-recycle loop reclaims ultra-fine material, which helps optimize recoveries even further. SGML is continually improving on this process, which has so far lowered plant-gate costs from $397 per tonne in Q1 2024 to $349 per tonne in Q1 2025. Additionally, SGML's CIF costs, including freight and royalties, fell 17% to $458 per tonne. These reductions stem largely from the proprietary DMS technology and process innovations. In other words, these are not one-off cost cuts but sustainable technology-led improvements, which I expect will pay off whenever Lithium's bull cycle starts again.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Moreover, SGML also reduced its short-term trade-finance debt by 42% between 2Q 2024 ($101 million) and 1Q 2025 ($59 million). This basically drove down interest per tonne from $36 to $17. This leaner financing, coupled with positive cash gross margins of 35%, has trimmed finance expenses by nearly $19 per tonne. So, fundamentally speaking, it seems SGML is actually delivering on the metrics they can control, which has definitely softened the hit from lower realized prices.

Even in the bearish scenario at $9,000 per tonne of lithium carbonate, SGML's ultra-low cost position and disciplined capex phasing should keep it comfortably cash-positive and within its debt covenants. However, if prices slide even further, to say, $8,000 per tonne or below, things would look worse and may force management to cut production. Alternatively, they could accelerate off-take prepayments at locked-in floors or postpone Plant 2 drawdowns to preserve liquidity.

However, I still believe we're probably near the bottom of the Lithium cycle in 2025. After all, factors like China's EV purchase subsidies that incentivize automakers and battery makers to restock lithium should help prop up spot prices. And if my bull case plays out, 2026 should be a much better year for the sector as a whole. Of course, that's a macro factor, and no one can predict the future with certainty. But at this point, I feel the balance of probabilities favors 2026 being a much more positive year for Lithium prices.

Now, from a valuation perspective, SGML currently trades at a $611.9 million market cap. As of Q1 2025, they hold $31.1 million in cash and equivalents, against total debt (net of accrued interest) of $165.3 million. So, I estimate their EV at about $746.1 million. Similarly, their EBIT reached $6.8 million for the quarter despite all the macro negatives.

This is a major positive swing from the quarterly EBIT loss a year ago in Q1 2024 of -$0.3 million. It also suggests an EBIT run rate of approximately $27.2 million at this level, which suggests they should be able to sustain operations at these depressed Lithium prices. Ultimately, Phase 2 construction is a variable SGML control, which I still think will pay off nicely if Lithium bounces going forward. However, Lithium prices will remain the wildcard, and this is their main risk at this point.

Still, I think a good valuation metric for now is their revenue. Seeking Alpha's dashboard on SGML indicates it will generate $395.7 million in annual revenues by 2026, implying a forward EV/S multiple of 1.9. For comparison, their sector's median EV/S multiple is 1.7, so it seems SGML remains in line with the rest of its industry for the time being. In other words, I think SGML's stock appears to be tracking mostly this multiple. So, if we assume that by 2027 SGML's production ramps up quickly as management anticipates, and Lithium prices recover by then, the upside could still be significant at these levels.

Conclusion: A "Macro Buy"

So, in the end, I have to reiterate my "Strong Buy" on SGML because the company itself has delivered on what it promised so far. Unfortunately, the macro factors haven't helped, but the company seems to be in a great position to reap the rewards by the time the next Lithium bull cycle kicks in. Just make sure you size your position accordingly, as Lithium can remain depressed for longer than anyone anticipates. And if the price declines a lot more, then SGML could get into trouble. But for now, that "distressed" scenario seems unlikely since management still has comfortable gross margins and a few options to play before reaching critical levels.

data.title: Consensus Price Forecasts - U.S.-China Trade Pact Lifts Industrial Metals Prices
data.description:
S&P Global Commodity Insights discusses consensus price forecasts for industrial and precious metals, including platinum group metals, amid broader market trends.

A temporary deferral of steep US-China trade tariffs renewed demand optimism across industrial metals markets in May, but the upside was limited by lingering concerns over the impacts of still-evolving trade dynamics on global economic growth prospects. Gold prices retreated from recent highs as trade risks thawed but remained elevated as macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties continued to drive investors' flight to safety. Appetite for safe-haven assets continued to bolster the consensus price outlook for gold in 2025–26, while tariff-induced demand worries and pockets of oversupply weighed on price expectations for industrial metals.

After months of escalation, trade tensions between the US and China partially eased as the world's two largest economies reached an agreement on May 12 to temporarily back down from aggressive "reciprocal" tariffs for a period of 90 days. The US slashed its import duties on Chinese goods from 145% to 30%, while China lowered its tariffs on US products from 125% to 10%. However, the reprieve may prove fragile after both the US and China alleged violations of the agreement just weeks after it entered into effect.

The trade-weighted index of the US dollar found temporary support from the US-China tariff pause but ended the month 10.1% weaker year-to-date. Despite the recent winding down of US-China import levies, the ongoing volatility in the global trade landscape continues to fuel concerns over supply chains, consumption levels and global economic growth prospects. The US Federal Reserve in its May 6–7 meeting said it downgraded US GDP growth expectations for 2025 and 2026, as tariffs stoke rising risks of inflation and unemployment. Global GDP growth outlooks from S&P Global Ratings have also been lowered amid trade uncertainty. Purchasing managers' indexes in the US and China were in contractionary territory in May, signaling a slowdown in economic activity.

China has expanded efforts to stimulate its domestic economy amid mounting trade challenges. The People's Bank of China announced May 6 that it would cut the reserve requirement ratio for banks for the first time since September 2024 and reduce housing loan rates. However, evolving trade dynamics and a sluggish recovery in the domestic property sector continue to drive downside risks for China's economic growth outlook.

After rising above the $3,400 per ounce threshold in late April, the London Bullion Market Association gold price pulled back in May to average $3,277.99/oz. As a temporary pause in US-China bilateral tariffs signaled cooling trade tensions, COMEX-based hedging slowed, and net long positions on COMEX gold declined in May. Yet, with the trade landscape remaining volatile, worries persist over potential inflationary pressures and slower global economic growth. Macroeconomic risks combined with geopolitical turmoil to bolster safe-haven demand for gold. Physically backed exchange-traded fund inflows reached a record high in April amid a surge of inflows into Asian funds, while central bank gold purchases remained robust in March. Consensus target prices for gold have been upgraded 1.5% on average for the 2025–28 period and downgraded 0.1% for 2029.

Bullish fundamentals and US dollar weakness kept the silver price supported on either side of $33/oz in May. Industrial applications in clean energy technologies and electronics fuel demand optimism for silver, while supply levels are expected to remain in deficit for the fifth consecutive year in 2025. A reprieve in trade tensions contributed to upside momentum for the silver price, but gloomy global economic growth prospects limit support. Silver consensus price forecasts have been adjusted 0.6% higher on average across 2025–28 and 0.3% lower for 2029.

The platinum price reached a two-year high of $1,094.60/oz on May 23, supported by expectations for a persistent structural deficit. In its latest quarterly report, the World Platinum Investment Council noted a shortfall of 816,000 oz in the platinum market during the March quarter, the largest single quarterly deficit in six years. Robust investment demand amid tariff-induced uncertainties offset reduced consumption in the automotive and industrial sectors during the quarter. Although overall demand is expected to decline for full-year 2025, supply will remain constrained, keeping the market deficit intact for a third consecutive year. Market tightness is limiting the downside for the platinum consensus price estimates, which have been lowered 0.5% on average for the 2025–29 period. Meanwhile, the palladium price largely moved below $1,000/oz in May, and consensus price targets have been downgraded 1.1% on average over the five-year forecast horizon.

The London Metal Exchange three-month (LME 3M) copper price recovered to reach a monthly peak of $9,610.00 per metric ton on May 23 as relaxed US-China trade barriers signaled renewed opportunities for copper trade. However, slowing manufacturing activity in the US and China amid lingering concerns over potential tariff impacts, a sluggish China property market and elevated refined supply, particularly in the US, limited price support. As anticipation of potential tariffs on US imports of copper encouraged front-loading, shipments jumped 55.5% month over month in March. On May 22, COMEX stocks exceeded levels held at the LME for the first time since March 2022, subsequently narrowing the COMEX-LME cash price arbitrage. Meanwhile, China copper cathode imports fell 8% year-over-year January–April, as domestic refined copper output rose 5.6%. Refined supply is largely expected to remain in a surplus through the end of the decade, while a concentrate deficit is projected to widen through to 2035. A concentrate supply squeeze prevails now, with spot treatment charges (TCs) continuing to tread record low levels. Consensus price forecasts for copper have been downgraded 0.3% on average for 2025–28 and adjusted 3.0% higher for 2029.

The brief respite from US-China trade tensions likewise drove the LME 3M zinc price to a monthly peak of $2,765.00/t May 14, but still-evolving trade dynamics could jeopardize demand prospects. Front-loading efforts triggered by worries over impending tariffs drove US refined zinc imports 28% higher month over month in March. Yet, the cost implications of increased import duties have weighed on US and China manufacturing activity, as well as US consumer confidence. Net investor positions on LME zinc entered negative territory for the first time since March 2024 amid heightened expectations of slowing global economic growth. Regarding supply, support from a steady decline in LME and Shanghai Futures Exchange inventories was offset by an ongoing loosening of the concentrate market as China spot TCs for zinc extended gains in May. Consensus price outlooks for zinc were lowered 0.2% on average for the five-year forecast horizon.

A stubborn supply glut prevailed over short-lived support from easing US-China trade friction at the LME nickel market, where the 3M price averaged at $15,540.40/t in May. Amid a backdrop of a persistently oversupplied global primary nickel market, investor funds remained net short on LME nickel in May. Nickel stocks at the LME held near a four-year high of 200,000 metric tons during the month. LME inventories are expected to remain robust even as the anticipation of potential tariff hikes could encourage metal flow into the US. Meanwhile, exacerbating demand-side pressure from a tenuous tariff pause, the US Congress is deliberating a proposal to revoke incentives introduced by former President Joe Biden for the purchase of electric vehicles. Slowing EV uptake would adversely impact demand for nickel and other battery metals. Consensus price expectations for nickel have been downgraded 0.2% on average for 2025–29.

The LME 3M aluminum price rebounded to $2,528.50/t on May 14 on the heels of the US-China tariff pause, before slipping to $2,444.00/t on May 30 amid lackluster fundamentals. Inventories at the COMEX and SHFE have fallen 54.1% and 24.3%, respectively, in the year-to-date period through May 30. Meanwhile, LME stock levels improved as canceled warrants — representing metal that could be withdrawn from warehouses — shrank significantly. On the demand side, China exports of semi-manufactured products declined 6% year over year in January–April amid reduced operating rates and profit margins among downstream processors, while North American light-vehicle output projections have been lowered due to the impact of tariffs. Slowing global economic growth as increased tariffs materialize would further stifle demand and widen an already anticipated market surplus. Consensus price forecasts for aluminum have been downgraded 0.2% on average for 2025–27.

The Platts IODEX 62% Fe iron ore price was caught between a narrow range of $96-$101 per dry metric ton in May, with a brief rally to the monthly peak of $102.80/dmt on May 14 on the heels of the US-China bilateral tariff cut agreement. Platts is part of S&P Global Commodity Insights. US sector-specific tariffs on imports of steel, aluminum and automotives continue to drag on market sentiments, alongside a persistent steel oversupply in China. Steelmakers in China face increasing pressure to curtail production amid challenging market conditions, which include a sluggish recovery in the domestic property market and rising trade protectionism. Reduced China steel production would pressure iron ore demand, which is already reflected in January–April iron ore imports, dropping 23.5 million metric tons year over year. The expected global seaborne iron ore trade surplus should be less severe over the next few years, as transportation infrastructure build-out challenges in Guinea slow the supply ramp-up from the Simandou greenfield project. As a result, consensus price outlooks for iron ore have been upgraded 0.4% for 2025 but downgraded 0.3% on average for 2026–29.

The Platts-assessed European cobalt metal price held at $16.00 per pound throughout much of May, buoyed by the depreciating US dollar as the market awaits clarity surrounding the export ban implemented by the Democratic Republic of Congo. The DRC government has yet to announce next steps after the export ban that took effect Feb. 22 reached its three-month review timeline in late May, with some observers speculating on a transition to a quota mechanism. Aimed at addressing abundant supplies, the export ban has yet to significantly impact production levels; major producers such as CMOC Group Ltd. (OTCPK:CMCLF) and Glencore PLC (OTCPK:GLCNF) anticipate steady output. However, DRC cobalt exports to China were lower year over year during the March quarter. Concerns have been raised about the possibility of stockpiled material flooding the market should the ban be lifted. Although EV uptake across key markets was strong in April, longer-term demand prospects are at risk amid accumulating trade barriers, such as US auto tariffs and imminent import duties on critical minerals. The cobalt consensus price target is 0.2% lower for 2025 and unchanged for 2026–29.
